narration.default: You've seen those little white domes on the ceilings of shops and offices — motion sensors. The moment something moves under them they tell the alarm to go off. Today you'll build that same setup with a PIR motion sensor and a buzzer. Quiet room: silence. Something moves: alarm. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and change the mode to INPUT. INPUT means the Arduino is going to LISTEN on this pin, not send signals out. The PIR sensor — a tiny eye that sees body heat moving — will whisper HIGH on this pin when it spots movement.
Drag a second pinMode block below. Set pin to 9 and leave mode on OUTPUT — pin 9 will drive the buzzer that yells when the sensor sees movement.

steps.title: Make the decision: alarm or silence?

narration.default: Open the Control category and drag an if / else block into the loop section. The if branch will switch the buzzer ON when motion appears; the else branch will keep things quiet when nothing's moving.
Open Operators and drag an equals block into the if condition slot. We're going to ask: 'is the sensor reading equal to HIGH?' We need to build both sides of that question next.

From the Arduino category, drag a digitalRead block into the left side of the equals. Set its pin to 2 — that's where the PIR sensor is wired. digitalRead reports back HIGH or LOW depending on what the sensor is seeing right now.
Drag a HIGH reporter from the Arduino category and drop it on the right side of the equals. The full condition now reads: if digitalRead(2) equals HIGH — meaning the PIR has spotted movement.
Drag a digitalWrite into the if branch. Set pin to 9 and keep value on HIGH — the buzzer turns on the moment the PIR spots movement.

Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch. Keep pin on 9 but change value to LOW — when nothing's moving, the buzzer stays silent. Without this else, the buzzer might keep ringing after motion stops.
Open the Simulator and click the plus button. Choose PIR Motion, pick pin 2, and click Save.
Click the plus again and add a Buzzer on pin 9. Both parts of the alarm — the eye and the voice — are now wired up.
Click Start. Your program compiles and the PIR begins watching for motion.

Click the PIR sensor in the simulator to toggle motion. The buzzer sounds the moment motion appears and falls silent when it stops. Try this: add an LED on pin 8 and turn it on at the same time as the buzzer — a flashing red light is a classic alarm. Or wire a second PIR for a hallway-and-room combo.
